what is a virus made of what is a virus made of what is a virus made of what is a virus made of what is a virus made of A virus is a microscopic infectious agent composed of genetic material (either DNA or RNA) enclosed in a protein coat called a capsid. Some viruses also have an outer lipid envelope derived from the host cell's membrane, which contains viral proteins that help the virus enter new host cells. Unlike living organisms, viruses cannot reproduce on their own—they must infect a host cell and hijack its machinery to replicate. Viruses vary in structure and complexity, but all share the basic components of genetic material and a protective protein shell. Examples of viruses include influenza, HIV, and SARS-CoV-2 (the virus that causes COVID-19).

axon terminals or synaptic knob definition axon terminals or synaptic knob definition axon terminals or synaptic knob definition axon terminals or synaptic knob definition axon terminals or synaptic knob definition **Axon Terminals or Synaptic Knob Definition**    Axon terminals, also known as synaptic knobs or terminal boutons, are the small, bulb-like structures at the end of axons in neurons. These specialized endings form synapses with other neurons or target cells (such as muscles or glands). Axon terminals contain synaptic vesicles filled with neurotransmitters, which are released into the synaptic cleft—a tiny gap between the axon terminal and the receiving cell—to transmit nerve impulses. This process enables communication between neurons or between neurons and effector cells, playing a crucial role in nervous system function.
